source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 16.3 journald (pp. 314-315)

questions.q: In what form does journald store its logs, and are they persistent by default?
options: Binary format, and NOT persistent by default (kept in /run/log/journal/, lost on reboot)
questions.why: journald writes a binary journal; by default it is volatile (/run/log/journal/) and cleared on reboot - read it with journalctl.

questions.q: Which of these does journald record?
options: Boot log, kernel log and application logs
questions.why: journald captures boot, kernel and application logs (and services' stdout/stderr).

questions.q: In journald's priority numbering, which number is emerg and which is debug?
questions.why: Like syslog: 0 emerg, 1 alert, 2 crit, 3 err, 4 warning, 5 notice, 6 info, 7 debug.

questions.q: As with rsyslog, how does journald priority relate to detail and severity?
options: Higher priority = less information but more serious; lower priority = more information, less serious
questions.why: Higher priority records fewer, more serious entries; lower priority records more, lighter detail.
